1: Mōšeh ûŠĕmûʾēl — "Even if Moses and Samuel stood before me, my heart would not be moved"; Moses (Exodus 32:11-14, Numbers 14:13-20) and Samuel (1 Samuel 7:5-9) were the two supreme intercessors of Israel's history. Both had moved YHWH to relent before. The fact that even they are named as insufficient underlines the finality of the judgment. The MKT preserves the comparison because it is theologically essential — no human intercession can reverse this decree.
2: ʾăšer lammāwet lammāwet — "Those destined for death will fall to death"; the fourfold division — death, sword, famine, captivity — is a comprehensive catalogue of the covenant curses. The rhetorical structure (repetition of the noun: "to death to death, to the sword to the sword") is an emphatic Hebrew form. The MKT preserves the fourfold categories but renders them as natural English: "death... sword... famine... captivity."
3: ʾarbaʿ mišpāḥôt — "four kinds of destroyers"; the four agents are personified as mišpāḥôt (families/clans) of destruction. The MKT renders "four kinds of destroyers" rather than the more literal "four families" because the English idiom "families of destroyers" obscures the point. The progression from sword to dogs to birds to beasts represents the complete destruction and desecration of the dead.
4: Mĕnaššeh ben-Ḥizqiyyāhû — "because of Manasseh son of Hezekiah"; Manasseh's reign (2 Kgs 21) was the paradigm of apostasy that even Josiah's reform could not undo. The phrase "a terrifying sight to all the kingdoms of the earth" (zāʿăwâ lĕkōl mamĕlĕkôt hāʾārets) — the nation becomes a spectacle of divine judgment. The MKT's "terrifying sight" captures the word zaʿăwâ (horror, trembling).
5: mî-yāḥmōl ʿālayik — "who will feel sorry for you, O Jerusalem?" The three rhetorical questions (who will have compassion? who will grieve? who will stop?) expect the answer: no one. The compassion vocabulary: yāḥmōl (have compassion), yānûd (show grief/commiserate), yisôl lĕšālôm (ask about your welfare). All three forms of social concern are abandoned because the city had abandoned YHWH.
6: nāṭaštî ʾet-ʾaḥôr — "you keep going backward"; nāṭaš (go backward, draw back) in the niphal. The MKT renders "you keep going backward" — the progressive tense captures the ongoing nature of the retreat. Wāʾettî yādî ʿālayik wāʾašḥîtēk — YHWH stretching out his hand against Israel is the reversal of the Exodus (where the hand was stretched out for Israel).
7: wāʾezrēm bĕmizreh bĕšaʿărê hāʾārets — "I will winnow them with a fan in the gateways of the land"; the agricultural winnowing image: chaff separated from grain. Bikkaltî šĕkailtî ʾet-ʿammî — "I will strip them of their children (bānim) and destroy my people". The double verb (kibbeltu šikiltu) — bereave and destroy — is an intensive pair. The MKT follows MT's two-verb construction.
8: ʿātsĕmû-lî ʾalmĕnôtāyw mēḥôl yammîm — "their widows have become more numerous to me than the sand of the sea"; a striking reversal of the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 22:17 — descendants as sand). Here it is widows, not descendants, who are countless. "A destroyer at midday" (šōdēd baṣṣāhŏrāyim) — midday attack emphasizes the openness and completeness of the slaughter.
9: ʾummâ šibbĕʿat hāyĕlādîm — "the mother of seven gasps her last breath"; seven sons was the ideal of complete blessing in the ancient world (cf. Ruth 4:15, 1 Samuel 1:8). Her sun going down while it is still midday (bāʾ šimšāh bĕʿōd yômmām) inverts the natural order — blessing reversed mid-life.
10: ʾîš rîb wĕʾîš mādôn — "a man of contention and strife"; Jeremiah's first personal lament — the prophet curses his own birth (anticipating Jeremiah 20). The MKT preserves "to the whole world" (lĕkol-hāʾārets): Jeremiah's birth was a source of contention nationally, not just locally. "I have neither lent nor borrowed" — his financial innocence makes his social ostracism incomprehensible to him.
11: ʾim-lōʾ šĕrîtĕkā lĕṭôb — "Surely I will set what remains of you free for good purposes"; YHWH's assurance to Jeremiah in the midst of his lament. The MKT renders "what remains of you" — šĕʾêrîtĕkā suggests a remnant or what remains of Jeremiah's ministry. The promise that enemies will "appeal to you in the time of disaster" is the reversal of Jeremiah's current humiliation.
12: hăyĕrōaʿ barzel barzel mitstsāpôn — "Can iron shatter iron from the north — or even bronze?" A difficult verse; the MKT follows the reading that this refers to Babylon (the iron from the north) being an irresistible force. Some read it as a general proverb; the MKT rendering as a rhetorical question emphasizes the rhetorical force rather than a metaphysical claim.
13: ḥêlĕkā ûʾôtsĕrôtêkā lābaz ʾettēn — "Your wealth and your treasures I will hand over to plunder — free of charge"; lōʾ bĕmĕḥîr (not for a price) means without any economic benefit to Israel — the wealth simply flows out. The phrase "because of all your sins, throughout all your territory" (bĕkol-ḥaṭṭōʾtêkā ûbĕkol-gĕbûlêkā) is comprehensive in scope.
14: kî ʾēš qĕdaḥat bĕʾappî — "for a fire has been kindled by my anger, and it will burn against you"; the divine anger as consuming fire — a standard OT metaphor. The exile to "a land you have never known" (ʾĕrets ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿtā) is the ultimate disorientation: covenantal relationship depended on knowledge of YHWH and of the land; exile dissolves both.
15: yādaʿtā YHWH zĕkĕrēnî ûpĕqĕdēnî — "You know, O LORD; remember me and care for me"; the second lament — Jeremiah appeals to YHWH's omniscience as the ground for attention to his own need. "Do not in your forbearance take me away" (bĕʾerek ʾappĕkā ʾal-tiqqāḥēnî) — the prophet fears that YHWH's patience with the persecutors may come at the cost of the prophet's own life.
16: nimsĕʾû dĕbārêkā wāʾōklēm — "When I found your words, I devoured them"; ʾākal (eat/devour) applied to the divine word; cf. Ezekiel 2:8-3:3 (Ezekiel literally eats the scroll). The MKT's "devoured them" preserves the physical urgency — not leisurely reading but consuming the words. "Your word was my joy and the delight of my heart" (śāśôn wĕśimḥat lĕbābî): the word that causes Jeremiah such suffering is simultaneously his deepest joy.
17: lōʾ-yāšabtî bĕsôd-mĕśaḥăqîm — "I did not sit in the company of those who celebrate"; Jeremiah's separation from normal social life because of his prophetic call. "I sat alone (bādād) because your hand was on me" — bādād (alone, isolated) is the word used for the isolated leper (Leviticus 13:46) and for Jerusalem destroyed (Lamentations 1:1: "How desolate she sits, the city once so full of people"). Prophetic isolation as anticipatory solidarity with the exiled city.
18: lāmâ hāyâ kĕʾēbî nĕtsaḥ ûmakkātî ʾănûšâ — "Why is my pain unending and my wound incurable?" The daring accusation that follows: "Are you really going to be to me like a deceptive stream, like waters that fail?" (hĕyôt tihyeh lî kĕʾakzāb). ʾAkzāb is a wadi that appears full in winter but dries up in summer — the most pointed image for unreliable presence. The MKT's "deceptive stream" preserves the shocking accusation that YHWH might be like the failed water-sources of the drought.
19: ʾim-tāšûb wĕʾăšîbĕkā — "If you return, I will restore you"; the double-šûb construction: Jeremiah's return (repentance/reorientation) is met by YHWH's returning him to standing. Lĕpānay taʿămōd — "you will stand before me" — the prophetic standing-before-YHWH is restored. "If you speak what is precious rather than what is worthless" — the prophet's speech must be reoriented before his commission is recommissioned.
20: lĕḥômâ nĕḥōšet bĕṣûrâ — "a fortified bronze wall" — YHWH's recommissioning of Jeremiah as an impregnable wall of bronze. This parallels the original commissioning of 1:18 where he was made "a fortified city, an iron pillar, a bronze wall." The recommissioning after Jeremiah's complaint restores him to his original calling with the same protective metaphor.
21: wĕhiṣṣaltîkā miyyad rāʿîm — "I will deliver you from the power of the wicked"; the final assurance completing the recommissioning. Mikkap ʿārîṣîm ʾepĕddekā — "I will rescue you from the grasp of the ruthless"; ʿārîṣ (ruthless one, violent tyrant) describes Jeremiah's opponents. The divine rescue is YHWH's responsibility, not something Jeremiah must earn.
